similar_questions.title: Determine Whether Matrix Can Be Obtained By Rotation

Sorry for the bad images. I am terrible at it.

Transpose: Basically run a line from top left to bottom right as shown. Flip the 2 parts around the line.

Reverse: Flip the whole image around a line to the left or right.

discussion_posts.name: jerry_nk
discussion_posts.content:
90 = transpose + reverse row
180 = reverse row + reverse column
270 = transpose + reverse col

discussion_posts.name: SaurabhKapade
discussion_posts.content: In this question only two things to do-\n1. Find the transpose of the matrix\n2. Reverse the rows of the matrix

discussion_posts.name: StefanPochmann
discussion_posts.content:
def rotate(matrix)
      matrix.replace(matrix.reverse.transpose)
    end

I like Ruby's wordy style.

discussion_posts.name: dkode
discussion_posts.content:
Diagram:

![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/95016c57-f112-4e79-8be7-0f21c814c800_1630583198.7966883.png)

# M[i,j] --> M'[j,N-1-i]

We know that transpose relation is 
#### M[i,j]= M[j,i]
#### 

But the relation we got is slightly differernt

Think how to convert
M[i,j] to M[N-1-i,j]

This is equivalent to flipping the matrix horizontally.

M[i,j] to M[N-1-i,j]

Transpose (M[N-1-i,j]) --> M'[j,N-1-i]

Transpose and flipping are linear operation

Similary you can solve other cases like 90 degree, 180 degree, 270 degree etc

discussion_posts.name: charlesxiao2
discussion_posts.content:
This post is for rotating the matrix anticlockwise. Sorry, when I post it I didn't notice it. But please read it, because I guarantee you can understand the thought process and came up with your solution for clockwise rotation.

So, we are required to rotate it in place. 

Give a matrix, there are several operations can be done in matrix in place:

translation, and swap symmetrically(mirror against diagnal), and probaly some unexpected in place operations.

We are trying to break the rotation operation into these operations that are know to carry on easily in place.

For swap symmetrically, given coordinate (a,b), it will be swapped to (b,a) if following the upper left and buttom right diagnal as mirror.
For translation, given coordinate (a, b), we are able to traslate it to (a, whatever) or (whatever, b) with ease.

If rotated anticlockwise, element at (a,b) will be transfered to the new coordinate of (s-b-1 ,a) with s being the length or width of the matrix.
We are trying to find out the in place operations needed in order to transform the coordinate from (a, b) to (s-b-1, a), with some fundumantal operations' coordinate transformation characteristics in mind.

we are trying to fill in the logic path between (a, b) to (s-b-1, a) using the fundamental operations.

Let's try to fill the logic break reversely:
to get to (s-b-1, a) from (a, b), notice a is swapped, we guess the last operation may be mirror against diagnal. so the path now becomes:
(a,b) -> (a, s-b-1) -> (s-b-1, a)//the last mirror can be easily done in place

Now we try, how to get to (a, s-b-1) from (a, b) using fundamental in place operations? Well, draw a matrix, and you find out that this is not translation, but some swapping too. Don't worry, you see the operations does not change the row coordinate, so it can be done in place with ease. And you know at this point you design the algorithm for this problem.

This is how the logic flows trying to design the in place algorithm to rotate n*n matrix.
Basically, we are trying to break down the operations into easy atomic in place operations. How to break down? Analyze the coordinate characteristics with some basic patterns in mind.

discussion_posts.name: chris3109692
discussion_posts.content: The complexity analyses in the solution is incorrect. For a NxN matrix, the complexity is O(N^2), but our data size is N^2 by itself, hence the proper way to denote complexity should be O(n). Note big O is always measured against the data size, not the dimension size.
